document.title: Digital radiography: optimization of image quality and dose using multi-frequency software
productModels: Canon DR with CXDI-50 C detector
standards: European guidelines on quality criteria for diagnostic radiographic images in pediatrics

sourceSummary.oneLine: A Pediatric Radiology original article evaluating whether Canon MLT(S) multi-frequency processing can reduce dose while maintaining digital radiography image quality in pediatric-equivalent femur phantom examinations.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Literature evidence for clinical evaluation or dose/image-quality optimization rationale; not an official FDA regulatory document.
keyPoints: The study used a Canon DR system with CXDI-50 C detector and MLT(S) MFP version 7.0.
MLT(S) optimized images maintained diagnostic image quality at a 61% dose reduction in the anthropomorphic phantom experiment.
Images of diagnostically acceptable quality supported an 88% dose reduction compared with the reference image.
The authors concluded that optimization of image processing parameters can enable significant dose reduction without significant loss of image quality.
The CDRAD technical phantom was not recommended for optimizing software parameters without anthropomorphic phantom assessment by experienced observers.

entities.name: H. Precht
entities.evidence: H. Precht & O. Gerke & K. Rosendahl & A. Tingberg & D. Waaler

entities.name: Canon DR with CXDI-50 C detector
entities.evidence: All radiographs were produced using a Canon DR system with the CXDI-50 C detector and MLT(S) MFP version 7.0.

entities.name: MLT(S) MFP version 7.0
entities.evidence: All radiographs were produced using a Canon DR system with the CXDI-50 C detector and MLT(S) MFP version 7.0.

entities.name: CDRAD 2.0
entities.evidence: 3,500 images taken of a technical contrast-detail phantom (CDRAD 2.0) provide an objective image-quality assessment.

entities.name: Artinis
entities.evidence: The CDRAD images were assessed with the CDRAD analysis software version 2.0 from Artinis.

entities.name: Canon Inc.
entities.evidence: Canon Inc. (2008) CXDI Image Processing Software MLT(S) User’s Manual. Japan

entities.name: ICRP
entities.evidence: ICRP (2004) Managing patient dose in digital radiology. ICRP Publication 93.

entities.name: European Commission
entities.evidence: European Commission (1996) European guidelines on quality criteria for diagnostic radiographic images in pediatrics.

concepts.name: Digital radiography optimization
concepts.evidence: Digital radiography (DR) has shown great potential for improving image quality and/or reducing radiation dose due to new post-processing techniques, detector design and new ways of optimizing technical parameters.

concepts.name: Multi-frequency processing
concepts.evidence: Multi-frequency processing (MFP) is a relatively new software tool for DR systems.

concepts.name: MLT(S) image processing
concepts.evidence: The aim of our study was to examine whether the use of multi-Laplacian transformation sub-band pyramidal enhancement-technology (MLT[S]) may allow dose reduction without significant loss of image quality.

concepts.name: Dose reduction
concepts.evidence: Optimal image-quality was maintained at a dose reduction of 61% with MLT(S) optimized images.

concepts.name: ALARA
concepts.evidence: optimized images allowed for exposures as low as 2 mAs (compared to the standard 16 mAs), emphasizing the importance of optimizing digital radiographs according to ALARA.

concepts.name: Visual Grading Analysis
concepts.evidence: Assessment of the images from the anthropomorphic phantom was carried out using relative visual grading analysis (VGA).

concepts.name: Contrast-detail phantom
concepts.evidence: A conventional contrast-detail radiography phantom (CDRAD) was used as technical phantom.

concepts.name: Entrance Skin Dose
concepts.evidence: Entrance Skin Dose (ESD) was reduced from 311 to 125 μGy (61% reduction).

evidence.claim: The article was received on 1 November 2011, revised on 21 February 2012, and accepted on 1 March 2012.
evidence.evidenceText: Received: 1 November 2011 / Revised: 21 February 2012 / Accepted: 1 March 2012
evidence.pageTarget: Digital radiography: optimization of image quality and dose using multi-frequency software

evidence.claim: The study examined whether MFP software reduces radiation dose without compromising femur DR quality in 5-year-old-equivalent phantoms.
evidence.evidenceText: Objective To examine whether the use of MFP software reduces the radiation dose without compromising quality at DR of the femur in 5-year-old-equivalent anthropomorphic and technical phantoms.

evidence.claim: The study used 110 anthropomorphic phantom images and 3,500 technical phantom images.
evidence.evidenceText: Two different image sets were produced and analyzed, one including 110 radiographs of an anthropomorphic phantom, and one including 3,500 radiographs of a technical phantom.

evidence.claim: All radiographs were produced on a Canon DR system with CXDI-50 C detector and MLT(S) MFP version 7.0.
evidence.evidenceText: All radiographs were produced using a Canon DR system with the CXDI-50 C detector and MLT(S) MFP version 7.0.

evidence.claim: The exposure index value REX was recorded for each exposure.
evidence.evidenceText: For each exposure, the ROI was manually set to cover precisely the same anatomical region in the image, and the REX-value was recorded to detect any bias caused by the operator, technique or generator.

evidence.claim: The reference image was exposed at 16 mAs and processed with optimized MLT(M) parameters equivalent to clinical practice.
evidence.evidenceText: The reference image was exposed at 16 mAs, corresponding to the optimal image quality and processed with optimized MLT(M) parameters equivalent to the application in clinical practice.

evidence.claim: MLT(S) optimized images maintained optimal image quality at a 61% dose reduction.
evidence.evidenceText: Optimal image-quality was maintained at a dose reduction of 61% with MLT(S) optimized images.

evidence.claim: MLT(S) provided an 88% dose reduction for images considered diagnostically acceptable.
evidence.evidenceText: Even for images of diagnostic quality, MLT(S) provided a dose reduction of 88% as compared to the reference image.

evidence.claim: Entrance Skin Dose was reduced from 311 μGy to 125 μGy at the 61% reduction level.
evidence.evidenceText: Entrance Skin Dose (ESD) was reduced from 311 to 125 μGy (61% reduction).

evidence.claim: At diagnostically acceptable quality, effective dose was reduced from 79 μSv to 4 μSv.
evidence.evidenceText: The reduction was 88% and the effective dose was reduced from 79 μSv to 4 μSv.

evidence.claim: Dose was the only statistically significant factor across all readers in the anthropomorphic phantom analysis.
evidence.evidenceText: To summarize, the dose parameter was the only statistically significant factor for all readers.

evidence.claim: For the technical phantom, dose and noise reduction were significant factors in InvIQF analysis.
evidence.evidenceText: In total, two significant factors were found at the 5% level, namely dose (mAs) and noise reduction.

evidence.claim: The authors stated that CDRAD phantom results contradicted anthropomorphic phantom results and CDRAD cannot be recommended for optimizing software parameters.
evidence.evidenceText: The results from the CDRAD studies contradicted the results obtained with the anthropomorphic phantom. Thus the CDRAD phantom cannot be recommended for optimizing software parameters although it has been used for optimization in other studies.

evidence.claim: The authors concluded that MLT(S) software supported lower mAs while maintaining optimal image quality in pediatric-equivalent femur phantom examinations.
evidence.evidenceText: The study showed great opportunities in the MLT(S) software as the anthropomorphic experiments of the project supported the hypothesis that multi-frequency processing software in DR maintain optimal image quality at lower mAs in pediatric-equivalent phantom examinations of the femur.

reviewFlags.reason: The source text is a journal article and literature reference, not an official FDA regulatory document, despite authoritySignal indicating FDA.
No source text mentions the productSignal HAD/A/F 1417-1717; product linkage to the metadata product is not established in the document.
No formal document revision or version number is present for the article; only received, revised, accepted, and copyright year information is present.
OCR/text extraction contains minor quality artifacts such as hyphenated line breaks and apparent symbol substitutions in statistical values.
